item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like talking animals skips, but I dislike compulsion skips even more. It rarely adds anything and is just overdone. Redacting the band members names crossed the line for me into joke skip territory, while the line about staff wishing to use 4909-2 for personal use is lolfoundation.

There are also odd bits which I cannot tell if they are typos, or intentional. Like saying the octopus won't let people be removed, but then it says they retrieve people. The article suggests that people don't try to leave because of the octopus or fear of drowning, but then it turns out to be compulsive. Something appearing to be indestructible is weird phrasing. That suggests it 'looks' indestructible, rather than some testing has shown it to be indestructible.

Those issues aside, I'm not feeling the hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The header at the bottom is ugly and the constant blackboxing is just really annoying. The story is boring and it just doesn't read well or look good.

Overall, it's not a very good article.

ETA: Jeez, that image is so huge that it's taking its sweet time on mobile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn’t really buy the second document section and the whole thing really reads like a funny animal and a grim dark article inexpertly mashed together. Both parts are weak, with the first focusing too much on processes like breathing underwater and the chemical structure without telling a compelling story and the second half being underdeveloped and more than a little implausible.

Also there is absolutely no reason for that first image to be almost five mb that’s ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't hate it, but it could be better.

Sadly, I'm going through these and then more S IV, so I can't help much but here.

+ show block
– hide block

the prose needs work at points.

I vaguely remember 1485, not really minding that you use it. Other people got to do it, it would be unfair to downvote you for taking your turn.

concepts, historical events, and/or people (most especially ones prominent in popular culture) are retroactively removed from the current timeline and “converted” into anomalous objects/phenomenon.

Neat.

Reshef-Class reality bender is most likely responsible

bleh. yet another eldrich god. Let it be weird cause its weird.

Thinking about it, +1. You made me smile at least one time, and I wanted to finish reading it. Could be better, but let it live.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said, this feels like two SCPs woven into one. I think you should either split them (the octopus+submarine already work together greatly) or cut some of the fat here.

SCP-1485 is really unnecessary, and even detrimental to this story I believe. We have a bajillion parallel universes in the canon, why do we need to check on the normie one to realize the Beatles have gone missing? Does it mean literally every other universe we know of also doesn't have them (so is 1485 really the "correct" one in having them)? No, nevermind that, why does the Foundation still have people in SCP-1485 if agents used to desert all the time?

I also don't really like how the Keter reveal was more "told" than "shown". I'd like to see the Hindi woman in the interview throw more references to Beatles's lyrics so the reader would go "hold up" and realize something was going on; bonus points if Agent Nandasiri didn't catch the references, so we'd know something (like a whole band lmao) was amiss.

If your intent with this article was to surprise the reader with the realization that the Beatles don't exist anymore (this is important because they did exist), I think you should try another approach. For example, maybe the inhabitants of the kelp garden/submarine still know about the Beatles (this one would let the Keter version be kept). Maybe the Foundation never really cracks open the mystery of this one SCP, but the readers realize what's going on. Or maybe the octopus is Ringo. Anything but the heavy crossover exposition you went with here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanna say: looks like you want to help the author with this, but you arent really. You say you like it but not what you like, you say they need to change "the base" but not what that base is or in what way to change it. This tells the author nothing.

Of course you are not obligated to tell the author anything, but since it seems youre trying to help them I thought I'd note this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 4.9 MB file for image that small is ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You were doing great until I refreshed the page! The effects of the updated SCP seem so generic. OOH! it changes things anomalous but this time BEATLES RELATED! I'd rather more interviews or something, it just feels like you made two completely different SCP's.

Kind Indian octopus that excretes soma is too good to do anything but upvote. You have a rose you just need to take out the thorns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really wish you didn't explain the Beatles reference. i'm a bit daft, but i can see "yellow submarine" and "beatles" and put two and two together. otherwise, this was a pretty good article. i'd really like to see a bit more expansion on the idea of a reality bender turning pop culture icons into anomalous objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted. I think this could really just work with the octopus by itself, taking out all the Beatles stuff. An octopus that wants to host for you, indefinitely, is a fine SCP by my standards. And doesn't remind me of the Beatles song at all, which isn't really about that.

The Beatles stuff could very well be another SCP. I like the idea of something as big as The Beatles having become unknown in this reality, and me as a reader only finding out midway through. But I'd like to see that developed, followed through until whatever they did to fix the problem and return to baseline reality.

If you're gonna go this way, having the octopus be the hook to the Beatles thing… I dunno, it's way ambitious, and hard to pull off. Not impossible, I guess, but I wouldn't know where to begin. Actually I do: first step is to remove the yellow submarine at the bottom of the sea. But after that, who knows…

The reason I upvote instead of downvoting, is that I cannot in good conscience downvote a recent article that in my opinion could work just by cutting out all the parts I didn't like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a lot of moving parts here that fail to come together in any satisfying way. The article was a slog to read through to get to the bait-and-switch ending, and when I got there… the expositiony "DO YOU GET THE REFERENCE!?" really didn't seem worth it. I think there's probably the seeds of at least two decent ideas in here, but the article definitely needs significant rewriting. Trim the description and condense the 1485 reference at the very least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noticed this didn't have too many eyes on it, and wasn't very highly rated either.

+ Line by Line
- Line by Line

[…] at coordinates ██° ██′ ██″ N ██° ██′ ██″ E.

Seeing blackboxes in the containment procedures is kind of a major red flag for many veteran users. How can you contain something if information about it is being hidden from you?

Due to risk of personnel loss

Due to the risk of personnel loss

two (2)

This is another red flag for SCP vets. Most scientific writing would simply have you write out numbers 20 and below, and use the characters for anything larger. So there would be eight fish and 45 dolphins, for example.

an estimated ██ humans

Why is this number redacted? You could give an approximation, like "two dozen", or something like that. There's no in-universe reason to hide this number from the researchers reading this. I suggest checking out Zen and the Art of DATA EXPUNGED for more information regarding proper redaction etiquette.

living organisms requiring oxygen are able to breathe normally

All aerobic organisms require oxygen, including marine animals. You might want to specify that specifically air breathing organisms can breathe down there to avoid confusion.

It is sapient, though unintelligent, […]

I think you'd be better served to just say that it is no more intelligent than most other non-anomalous octopus species.

Various chemical treatments, including [REDACTED] have been successful in alleviating the effects of SCP-4909-2 on individuals retrieved from SCP-4909, though symptoms of depression have followed in █ of the █ cases recorded to date.

Kind of beating the dead horse here, but again, in-universe personnel would need to know what to give their comrades who are affected by SCP-4909-2.

It is made of a material similar to steel, but appears to be indestructible.

This doesn't tell us anything. You're better off mentioning the indestructability in the previous sentence, not here on its own.

The following interview was obtained from █████ █████,

You really should come up with a name. Sites like https://www.behindthename.com/random/ are great for this. Much easier to identify with a person with a name than it is to identify with █████ █████.

being taken to SCPS-0215’s medical bay.

Best to avoid possessives when you can. Maybe something like "taken to the medical bay of SCPS-0215."

Dialogue translated to English from Hindi.

Sentence fragment. You need a "has been" in there.

Further research revealed that SCP-4909 and SCP-4909-3 bear a beyond-coincidental similarity to lyrics contained in The Beatles' songs “Octopus’s Garden” and “Yellow Submarine,” respectively.

Oh my god was this whole thing a giant Beatles pun? That actually almost makes me want to downvote right there. Reminds me of like those 4chan stories that get you hooked until the final line is something stupid like "and his name was john cena". I don't mind the subtle Beatles references of an octopus garden and a yellow submarine, but beating the reader over the head with it might not be the best idea.

Mobile Task Force MTF Omega-9 (“Lonely Hearts Club”)

Honestly this is tongue-in-cheek enough for me to be completely cool with it, but Christ that earlier stuff literally drawing a straight Beatles connection was too much.

I understand this is an atypical criticism of anyone […]

You might wanna restructure the collapsible to not be in quoteblocks so that notes like these can be. Just a personal stylistic thing, but that's ultimately up to you.

Overall, I have four major gripes with this:

  1. The needless and constant redaction is annoying, not gonna lie.
  2. Your description is HUGE. Trimming it down would improve the pacing dramatically.
  3. The ham-fisted delivery of the Beatles references killed any impact or realization that it otherwise might have had on my part.
  4. This is the big one I think. You relied way too heavily on SCP-1485 to tell your story. It's one thing to have crosslinks in the article. It's another entirely to have an article be the entire basis of your big reveal. I had to stop reading your piece to read 1485, and then come back when I was done, and that destroyed all of the pacing and tension you'd built up to that point, including the interview that took place just before it.

Given all of that, I've left a downvote. Hope this can be helpful for your future articles though!

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Currently, you have not sourced the image used in this article, and as such may be a violation of the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0). You have 24 hours to source the image used. If you have not sourced the image within the allotted time frame, or the source is noncompliant with the SCP wiki's license, then we'll have to take the image down.

EDIT: You have not sourced the image within 24 hours, and as such was susceptible to be taken down. However, after searching, we found that the image was in fact compatible with the wiki's license, originating from here, so it's allowed to stay. However, as a reminder, please source your images in the discussion thread when you upload an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reference is pretty obvious here without the addendum. I don't have a long winded critique, it just doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed reading this. However, please rework some of the base content in the sandbox. I really don't want for this to be deleted. Keep up your efforts.
Best of Luck,
Dr. P.
